beginnerGuide.title: Esports Manager 2026 Beginner Guide
beginnerGuide.intro: Esports Manager 2026 lets you start from scratch or manage an established organization from a pool of more than 325 real organizations. A safe opening focuses on roster legality, role coverage, staff, weekly training, morale, contracts, and reachable tournaments before expensive star signings.

steps.title: Choose Your Starting Path
steps.description: Create your own organization for a complete rebuild, or take over an existing team with players, reputation, and competitive expectations already attached. Review the starting budget, squad, staff, and tournament calendar before advancing time.

steps.title: Complete a Five-Player Starting Lineup
steps.description: The match roster needs five players, and recent official updates make short-handed AI teams fill missing places with role-aware signings. Confirm the lineup covers IGL, AWPer, Entry Fragger, Support, Lurker, and general Rifler duties without excessive role overlap.

steps.title: Negotiate the First Essential Contract
steps.description: Compare transfer cost, wage demand, contract term, current ability, role fit, and development value before sending an offer. Use free agents when the transfer budget is tight and keep enough budget for wages, staff, training, and later roster changes.

steps.title: Hire the Core Backroom Staff
steps.description: The game supports coaches, analysts, junior managers, media specialists, and other staff who contribute to performance and organization growth. Prioritize a coach for development and an analyst for match preparation, and match staff quality to club prestige.

steps.title: Set Weekly Training and Protect Morale
steps.description: Weekly plans affect skill growth and well-being, while team-building, psychology sessions, smart scheduling, and the Talk module support morale. Train the team's next competitive weakness instead of every attribute at once, and prepare a small group of maps first.

steps.title: Enter the First Tournament Cycle
steps.description: The calendar ranges from smaller competitions to major championships, with rankings, sponsorships, and organization visibility tied to long-term progress. Choose events that match the current roster level and use early matches to test the starting five, map preparation, and tactical roles.

steps.title: Follow the First-Season Route
steps.description: Use a simple sequence: legal roster, essential contract, core staff, weekly training, morale check, first tournament, then controlled upgrades. Keep a stable starting five long enough to evaluate role fit and review finances before each new tournament block.

quickTips: Do not spend immediately. First identify which positions, contracts, and staff roles are actually missing.
Do not sign several players for the same specialist role simply because their overall rating is high.
Avoid one marquee signing that consumes the budget but leaves the team without role balance or bench cover.
Develop promising players instead of replacing the entire roster after one event.
Do not treat every early loss as proof that the roster needs another expensive transfer.

playersTierList.title: Esports Manager 2026 Players and Roles Tier List
playersTierList.intro: The Esports Manager Database stores player skill, specialist attributes, roles, team assignment, and career earnings in the format used by Esports Manager 2026. The rating tier is a fast strength indicator, but a complete roster still needs IGL, AWPer, Entry Fragger, Support, Lurker, and reliable Rifler coverage.

tiers.label: Elite / Top-20
tiers.meaning: The highest displayed player tier, intended for elite names and top-20 level talent.
tiers.bestUse: Use as a franchise anchor, primary star, elite AWPer, or high-impact leader when the wage and transfer cost fit the organization.

tiers.label: Professional / Tier-1
tiers.meaning: Strong professional players suited to tier-1 competition and regular starting roles.
tiers.bestUse: Build most of a competitive core from A-tier players with complementary roles and attributes.

tiers.label: Semi-Pro
tiers.meaning: Semi-professional players who can provide affordable starters, bench depth, or development value.
tiers.bestUse: Target B-tier players when the budget needs role coverage, future growth, or a lower-cost replacement.

tiers.label: Amateur / Lower-Tier
tiers.meaning: The entry rating tier for amateur and lower-level players.
tiers.bestUse: Use for emergency depth, low-budget starts, custom databases, or long development projects.

roles.function: Fills the leadership slot while contributing utility, decision-making value, and late-round stability.
Creates long-range impact and gives the tactical system a dedicated primary sniper.
Takes early fights, creates space, and begins attacking sequences.
Uses utility, enables teammates, and stabilizes executes and retakes.
Controls space away from the group, punishes rotations, and handles isolated late-round situations.
Fills flexible positions, trades teammates, and supports both aggressive and controlled plans.

scoutingTransfers.title: Esports Manager 2026 Scouting, Transfers and Contracts
scoutingTransfers.intro: Esports Manager 2026 uses a dynamic market with transfers, loans, player consent, contract negotiations, wages, role-aware squad building, and club prestige. The best deal is not always the highest-rated player; compare immediate impact with total cost and long-term development.

routes.bestUse: Completing a five-player lineup, adding low-cost depth, or signing a prospect without paying another club.
Buying a clear starter upgrade or filling a specialist role the free-agent pool cannot cover.
Adding short-term quality when the club cannot afford a permanent transfer.
Giving bench or young players competitive time while preserving ownership.
Protecting a core starter, retaining a high-potential player, or preventing a replacement search.

routes.commitment: High wage and major roster expectations
routes.bestUse: Providing immediate championship impact in a role the team is built to support.

routes.commitment: Wage, term, training time, roster space
routes.bestUse: Building value across several seasons through weekly training and regular playing time.

scoutingTransfers.note: Official updates adjusted transfer-price formulas, scale wages more closely to player rating, and expanded new-player inflow. Each club can have up to two players loaned in and two loaned out.

tacticsSimulation.title: Esports Manager 2026 Tactics and Match Simulation
tacticsSimulation.intro: Simulation Mode places the manager inside a semi-3D match where tactical calls, rotations, map bans, buy logic, sniper roles, and live events affect the series. Start with a small set of repeatable plans and change one element at a time when the match pattern demands it.

entries.heading: Build the Plan Around the Starting Five
entries.content: Assign a clear IGL, primary AWPer, Entry Fragger, Support, Lurker, and flexible Rifler coverage before choosing tactics. The plan should use each player's strongest specialist attributes instead of forcing every player into the same style.
entries.template: IGL/Rifler + AWPer + Entry Fragger + Support + Lurker

entries.heading: Prepare a Small Map Pool and Veto Plan
entries.content: Weekly training can target specific maps, and official updates have improved the AI map-ban algorithm and per-map calculations. Build one reliable first pick, one secondary option, and one safe fallback before trying to prepare every map.
entries.template: Ban the least prepared map, protect the best-trained map, and keep a practiced fallback.

entries.heading: Balanced Default Tactic
entries.content: Use a controlled opening that lets the Entry Fragger create space, the Support prepare utility and trades, the AWPer hold a long angle, the Lurker control a separate lane, and the IGL or Rifler connect the group.
entries.template: One entry action, one support package, one AWP angle, one lurker route, one flexible trader.

entries.heading: Fast Pressure Tactic
entries.content: Use a faster plan when the opponent repeatedly gives away early space or rotates too slowly. The Entry Fragger begins the move, a Rifler follows for the trade, Support utility clears the route, and the Lurker blocks the rotation.
entries.template: Entry first, trader second, Support utility behind, AWPer watching the response, Lurker cutting the rotate.

entries.heading: Economy and AWP Decisions
entries.content: The match engine includes buy logic, weapon roles, and decisive-round economy behavior. Recent updates reworked buy logic and enabled double-AWP setups by assigning the second weapon to the secondary sniper.
entries.template: Keep full-buy rounds coordinated and use double AWP only when two assigned sniper roles justify the cost.

entries.heading: React During Simulation Mode
entries.content: Matches unfold in real time with dynamic events, live casters, tactical rules, timings, rotations, post-plant situations, and manager decisions. Change one variable at a time so the result can be read clearly.
entries.template: Adjust tempo, target area, or player task separately instead of replacing the entire tactic after one round.

entries.heading: Adapt Between Maps
entries.content: Use the first map to identify which roles, timings, and economy choices are working. Keep successful structure, replace the failing detail, and use the next veto or tactical setup to move the series toward the roster's strengths.
entries.template: Keep the stable core, change the weak map plan, and protect morale with clear player roles.

trainingStaff.title: Esports Manager 2026 Training, Morale and Staff Guide
trainingStaff.intro: Esports Manager 2026 connects player development with morale and workload management. Weekly training plans, psychology sessions, team-building activities, conversations, staff recruitment, and tournament scheduling all contribute to the long-term condition of the roster.

cards.title: Weekly Training Plans
cards.description: Create weekly plans that target skill growth while also protecting player well-being. Training should reflect the team's current weaknesses, available preparation time, and upcoming tournament schedule.
cards.action: Use focused development during open weeks and reduce the workload when several competitive matches are scheduled close together.

cards.title: Team Building
cards.description: Team-building activities help managers address the human side of roster development. They are most useful when new players join, results decline, or relationships inside the lineup begin to weaken.
cards.action: Include team-building work after roster changes instead of expecting newly signed players to perform as a complete unit immediately.

cards.title: Psychology Sessions
cards.description: Psychology sessions support the mental health and motivation systems included in the game. They provide an alternative to treating every performance problem as a lack of mechanical skill.
cards.action: Use psychological support when morale or motivation falls rather than increasing training intensity automatically.

cards.title: Talk Module
cards.description: The Talk module allows direct conversations with players and staff. Managers can learn about individual needs, motivations, complaints, and conflicts before those issues damage the wider team.
cards.action: Check conversations regularly and respond to concerns before important tournaments or contract negotiations.

cards.title: Workload and Scheduling
cards.description: Training must be balanced against travel, tournament dates, match preparation, and player well-being. A crowded calendar can make an aggressive training plan harder to sustain.
cards.action: Review the tournament calendar before confirming the weekly plan and leave room for recovery around important events.

cards.title: Coaches
cards.description: Coaches are part of the full support team available to an organization and are a central staff choice for managers focused on player and team development.
cards.action: Prioritize a coach when the organization needs structured development rather than relying only on expensive transfers.

cards.title: Analysts
cards.description: Analysts strengthen the competitive support structure around the roster. They are designed to contribute to preparation and the wider performance operation alongside coaches and managers.
cards.action: Add analyst support as the team begins entering stronger tournaments and requires a more complete competitive staff.

cards.title: Junior Managers
cards.description: Junior managers expand the management structure behind the main roster. They become more valuable as the number of players, staff members, contracts, and scheduled events increases.
cards.action: Hire junior management support after the organization grows beyond a small first-season operation.

cards.title: Media Specialists
cards.description: Media specialists support the organization-building side of the game, where visibility, branding, sponsorships, and influence matter alongside tournament results.
cards.action: Invest in media staff when the club is ready to turn competitive progress into stronger visibility and commercial growth.

sponsorsFinance.title: Esports Manager 2026 Sponsors, Finance and Organization Growth
sponsorsFinance.intro: Every contract, signing, sponsorship, and staffing decision affects the wider organization. The safest first-season approach is to establish a functional roster, protect operating funds, develop affordable talent, and expand commercial operations only when the club can support the added costs.

stages.actions: Review the available budget before signing players or staff. Reserve enough money for contracts, salaries, tournament operations, and unexpected roster problems.
stages.risk: Spending most of the starting budget on one recognizable player before the rest of the organization is functional.

stages.actions: Compare a player's competitive value, role, contract demands, and effect on the complete lineup. Build a balanced roster instead of collecting several expensive names with overlapping roles.
stages.risk: Creating a top-heavy wage structure that limits future transfers and causes dissatisfaction across the squad.

stages.actions: Begin with staff who directly support development, preparation, player management, or organization operations. Add specialized roles as the tournament schedule and business workload increase.
stages.risk: Hiring a large backroom team before the club has stable competitive income.

stages.actions: Scout players, negotiate contracts, and consider buying, selling, or loaning talent according to the club's actual needs. Developing promising players can be safer than immediately pursuing established superstars.
stages.risk: Paying a major transfer fee without checking role balance, morale, contract cost, and the remaining budget.

stages.actions: Use sponsorship opportunities to strengthen the business side of the organization. Competitive results and growing visibility provide a stronger base for long-term commercial development.
stages.risk: Treating sponsorship income as unlimited transfer money while ignoring recurring salaries and operating costs.

stages.actions: Invest in media activity and organization branding as the team becomes more established. Visibility supports the wider goal of increasing the club's influence across the esports scene.
stages.risk: Expanding branding operations faster than the competitive team and financial base can support.

stages.actions: Use improved results and stronger finances to upgrade the roster, expand staff, enter larger competitions, and pursue more ambitious sponsorship opportunities.
stages.risk: Replacing a successful structure with multiple high-cost signings after a single strong tournament.

downloadDemo.title: Esports Manager 2026 Download, Demo and PC Requirements
downloadDemo.intro: Esports Manager 2026 launched for PC on July 6, 2026. The full game is sold through Steam and the indie.io store, while a separate free Steam demo lets players test performance and the core management systems before buying.

specs.label: Release Date
specs.details: The full PC version was released by Neurona Games and indie.io.

specs.label: Available Platforms
specs.details: The official release is available on PC through Steam and the indie.io store.

specs.label: Free Demo
specs.details: Open the Esports Manager 2026 Demo page, sign in to Steam, select Install Demo, choose an installation location, and launch it from the Steam Library.

specs.label: Demo Release Date
specs.details: The demo is listed as a separate free Steam application and is updated with changes from the release version.

specs.label: Full Game Price
specs.details: A 20% introductory Steam discount is listed through July 20, 2026. Final prices vary by region, currency, taxes, and store.

specs.label: indie.io Store Delivery
specs.details: After a successful purchase, the activation key and receipt are sent to the checkout email address.

specs.label: Operating System
specs.details: A 64-bit processor and operating system are required.

specs.label: Processor
specs.value: Intel Core 2 or AMD Athlon 64 X2
specs.details: The official minimum listing targets older dual-core processor families.

specs.label: Memory
specs.details: Both the minimum and recommended listings show 2 GB of system memory.

specs.label: Graphics
specs.value: Intel GMA X4500, NVIDIA GeForce 9600M GT, or AMD/ATI Mobility Radeon HD 3650
specs.details: The graphics listing requires at least 256 MB VRAM and DirectX 11 capability.

specs.label: DirectX
specs.details: The official requirement table lists DirectX Version 10.

specs.label: Storage
specs.details: Keep additional free space available for Steam installation files, saves, and updates.

specs.label: Supported Languages
specs.value: 11 languages in the full Steam release
specs.details: English, French, German, Spanish, Polish, Russian, Portuguese, Simplified Chinese, Swedish, Turkish, and Ukrainian are listed.

specs.label: Older PC Compatibility
specs.details: A computer meeting the listed processor, RAM, graphics, DirectX, and storage requirements should be able to attempt the game. The free demo is the quickest way to test performance.

updatesPatchNotes.title: Esports Manager 2026 Updates and Patch Notes
updatesPatchNotes.intro: The first post-launch updates focused on transfer-market behavior, match simulation, training balance, wages, roster generation, localization, and problems affecting existing saves. Steam normally downloads these patches automatically before the game launches.

entries.summary: A small stability and transfer-realism update that repairs retired players stuck inside active squads.
changes: Retired players are removed from the locked roster state that prevented selling, releasing, or changing their contracts.
Existing affected saves repair the retired-player roster problem automatically when loaded.
Retired squad members no longer send wage-demand or playing-time complaints.
The career-start transfer freeze now also stops AI clubs from bidding for the user's players.
World-class players are less likely to join clubs far below their prestige level.
German menu labels, prompts, finance terms, and transfer actions received localization improvements.
entries.impact: Players with retired squad members should load the career normally so the automatic roster repair can run.

entries.summary: A major simulation, training, economy, transfer-market, localization, and database update.
changes: Fixed player pathing, navigation grids, map loading, and Counter-Terrorist rotations after the bomb is planted.
Improved AI map bans, decisive-round purchasing, tactic behavior, and double-AWP assignment.
Reduced ERS rating gains from matches, team comparisons, and tournaments.
Rebalanced training formulas and lowered maximum NewGen attributes while increasing player variety.
Rebalanced quick-match results and per-map individual rating calculations.
AI clubs wait 14 days after a rejected bid before returning with a higher offer.
Short-handed AI teams sign players according to required roles and fill incomplete rosters more quickly.
Starting and market wages were lowered, transfer values recalculated, and wages scale more closely with rating.
A player's wage drops by 30% after spending one month on the bench.
Career creation added an undismissable-manager option and a choice to freeze the AI transfer market.
Updated the team and player database with new rosters, transfers, and free agents.
entries.impact: Active careers receive the new training, wage, ERS, transfer, simulation, and player-generation rules after updating.

entries.summary: The first post-launch patch expanded player loans and fixed scouting, simulation, staff-training, audio, interface, and localization issues.
changes: AI clubs can loan players to and from one another instead of relying only on permanent transfers.
Clubs normally loan bench or young players while protecting starting stars.
Players can reject loans to clubs far below their level, with region and compatibility considered.
Each club can have up to two players loaned in and two players loaned out.
Players return with their original contracts intact when loans end, and managers can recall them early for a reduced fee.
Fixed scouting filter defaults, reset behavior, and the displayed number of matching players.
Corrected post-plant simulation behavior and rotation routes on multiple maps.
The appropriate localized caster is selected automatically, with fixes for commentary and round-MVP announcements.
Added the seasonal EMTV Top 20 countdown and Player of the Year presentation with season statistics.
entries.impact: Existing careers gain the expanded AI loan market, corrected staff training, simulation fixes, and seasonal EMTV awards.

faq.title: Frequently Asked
faq.subtitle: Everything you need to know about Esports Manager 2026

questions.question: What is Esports Manager 2026?
questions.answer: Esports Manager 2026 is a PC esports club management simulation developed by Neurona Games and published by indie.io, released on Steam on July 6, 2026. You scout players, negotiate contracts, manage training and morale, and control live match tactics.

questions.question: Where can I play Esports Manager 2026?
questions.answer: Esports Manager 2026 is available on PC through Steam and the indie.io store. A separate free Steam demo lets you test the core management systems before buying the full game.

questions.question: How much does Esports Manager 2026 cost?
questions.answer: The US Steam base price for Esports Manager 2026 is $19.99, with a 20% introductory discount listed through July 20, 2026. Final prices vary by region, currency, taxes, and store.

questions.question: Does Esports Manager 2026 include real teams and players?
questions.answer: Yes. Esports Manager 2026 includes more than 325 real organizations and a player database with skill, specialist attributes, roles, team assignments, and career earnings.

questions.question: Can I run Esports Manager 2026 on an older PC?
questions.answer: Yes. Esports Manager 2026 targets modest 64-bit hardware, with a minimum of 2 GB RAM, a dual-core processor, and 256 MB VRAM graphics. The free Steam demo is the quickest way to test performance before buying.

questions.question: Will Esports Manager 2026 receive updates?
questions.answer: Yes. The first post-launch patches for Esports Manager 2026 improved transfer-market behavior, match simulation, training balance, wages, roster generation, and localization. Steam normally downloads these updates automatically.
